standard_description: Solve addition and subtraction word problems, and add and subtract within 10, e.g., by using objects or drawings to represent the problem.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Task
Materials
Each pair of students needs: * One worksheet * Two markers of different colors * One pair of dice
Action
Student A rolls the two dice, finds the sum, and traces the number on the worksheet which corresponds to the answer with his/her marker. Student A then passes the dice to Student B who rolls both the dice, finds the sum and traces the correct number on the worksheet with his/her marker. Play continues this way until one of the numbers “wins” (i.e. all of the numbers of that quantity have been traced).
###IMAGE0###

Task
Adding two numbers to make an equation.
Materials
One pair of dice  per student
A recording sheet for the activity. For example:
###IMAGE0###
Action
The students roll the dice.  They record the numbers on the dice, one as the first addend and the other as the second addend in the equation, with numerals or dot pattern from the dice. They count all the dots and record the total in the equation.

Task
Materials
Each pair of students needs: * One worksheet * Two markers of different colors * One pair of dice
Action
Student A rolls the two dice, finds the sum, and traces the number on the worksheet which corresponds to the answer with his/her marker. Student A then passes the dice to Student B who rolls both the dice, finds the sum and traces the correct number on the worksheet with his/her marker. Play continues this way until one of the numbers “wins” (i.e. all of the numbers of that quantity have been traced).
###IMAGE0###

Problem 3
Pre-unit
There were 5 monkeys swinging in the tree.
Then 2 more monkeys came to join them.
How many monkeys are in the tree now?
Show your thinking using drawings, numbers, or words.

Problem 2
Pre-unit
There are 8 cars parked on the street.
Some cars are red and some are blue.
Show how many red and blue cars could be on the street.
Show your thinking using drawings, numbers, or words.

Task
Adding two numbers to make an equation.
Materials
One pair of dice  per student
A recording sheet for the activity. For example:
###IMAGE0###
Action
The students roll the dice.  They record the numbers on the dice, one as the first addend and the other as the second addend in the equation, with numerals or dot pattern from the dice. They count all the dots and record the total in the equation.

Task
Materials
Ten Flashing Fireflies by Philemon Sturges
10 frame and 10 counters per student
Activity
Begin with 10 counters off the 10 frame mat with the yellow side facing up to represent the fireflies in the sky. As you read the story, the students should move a yellow counter to the ten frame to represent the firefly in the jar. Stop several times during the story to allow the students time to talk with their partner about how many fireflies are in their jar and how many are in the sky. Ex. There are 3 fireflies in my jar and 7 fireflies are in the sky. That equals 10 fireflies all together. At the end of the story, let the students put the counters away to represent the fireflies flying away. As a group, have the students come together to write a class list of all the combinations for 10 that were made.

Task
Show the student 6 counters (small, flat objects). Ask the student to close his/her eyes. Hide some of the counters under a sheet of heavy paper. When the student opens his/her eyes, s/he determines how many were hidden based on the number of counters still showing.

tasks.task_text:
K.OA Ten Flashing Fireflies
Materials
Ten Flashing Fireflies by Philemon Sturges 
10 frame and 10 counters per student
Activity
Begin with 10 counters off the 10 frame mat with the yellow side facing up to represent the
fireflies in the sky. As you read the story, the students should move a yellow counter to the
ten frame to represent the firefly in the jar. Stop several times during the story to allow the
students time to talk with their partner about how many fireflies are in their jar and how
many are in the sky. Ex. There are 3 fireflies in my jar and 7 fireflies are in the sky. That
equals 10 fireflies all together. At the end of the story, let the students put the counters away
to represent the fireflies flying away. As a group, have the students come together to write a
class list of all the combinations for 10 that were made.
Illustrative 
Mathematics

K.OA What’s Missing?
Alignments to Content Standards:  K.OA.A.2
Task
Show the student 6 counters (small, flat objects). Ask the student to close his/her eyes.
Hide some of the counters under a sheet of heavy paper. When the student opens
his/her eyes, s/he determines how many were hidden based on the number of
counters still showing.
IM Commentary
Repeat the task 4-5 times per student.
This task may be repeated with a different number of counters.
Students should eventually be able to recognize the number of counters that are
showing and/or hidden without counting.
The words compose and decompose are used to describe actions that young students
learn as they acquire knowledge of small numbers by putting them together and taking
them apart. This understanding is a bridge between counting and knowing number
combinations. It is how instant recognition of small numbers develops and leads
naturally to later understanding of fact families.
Edit this solution
Solution
Students should look at how many counters are showing and determine how many
objects are hidden.
1
Illustrative 
Mathematics

K.OA What’s Missing?

Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International License .
2
Illustrative 
Mathematics

khan_exercises.title: Subtraction word problems within 10
khan_exercises.description: Practice solving word problems by subtracting small numbers (numbers 10 or less).

items.question_content:
AJ saw $9$ sharks.  
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d6d66279002845c45b9ec22ac9bfccbd.png)  
$5$ of them swam away. 

**How do we find the number of sharks that are left?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Jack had $10$ mice.  
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/afc7b1d1832fdf137e461430ec740bb6.png)  
$6$ mice ran away. 


**How do we find the number of mice that are left?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Ming put $7$ flowers on his porch.  
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e6ea164c12c285f7d6334865c32096e4.png)  
A rabbit ate $4$ of them. 

**How do we find the number of flowers that are left?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Dana saw $8$ foxes.  
$3$ of them ran away.

**How do we find the number of foxes that are left?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Carol saw $5$ horses.  
$2$ of them ran away. 

**How do we find the number of horses that are left?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Hal had $6$ cherries.  
His sister ate $3$ of them. 

**How do we find the number of cherries that are left?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Hal's sister ate $3$ away so we *subtract* $3$.

items.question_content:
There are $10$ monkeys in a tree.  
$8$ of the monkeys leave.

**How many monkeys are left?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

A tree had $6$ acorns.  
The wind blew $3$ acorns off.

**How many acorns are left?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Riley had $7$ apples in a basket.  
$2$ apples fell out.

**How many apples are left?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Mom bought $5$ bananas.  
Max ate $3$ of them.

**How many bananas are left?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

$8$ elephants were walking.  
$5$ of the elephants stopped to rest.

**How many kept walking?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

A daisy had $9$ ladybugs on it.  
$4$ of the ladybugs flew away.

**How many ladybugs are left?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

$3$ squirrels were running.  
$2$ of the squirrels stopped.

**How many squirrels kept running?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Shota had $10$ flowers. 
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bef5dca35ecf91c6a5f7f5aa4938860e9f54c049)

His rabbit ate $5$ flowers.

**How many flowers are left?**  
[[☃ input-number 1]] flowers

Mrs. Telly bought $3$ bananas.  
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c2c1df340c8bf89add3d6b80201e8fc0270c24ed)

She stepped on $2$ bananas. Yuck! 

**How many bananas are still good?**  
[[☃ input-number 1]] banana(s)

Diego bought $4$ apples.  
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/deea294986a46ddbc1571a49074754e2567cc952)

He gave $1$ apple away.

**How many apples does he have now?**  
[[☃ input-number 1]] apples

Lola had $6$ blocks of cheese.  
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/81d9627592e4cdc2b7c47c5a7a38429d99728701)

A mouse ate $4$ of them.
 
**How many are left?**  
[[☃ input-number 1]]

There were $10$ sharks at the reef.  
$2$ sharks swam away.

**How many sharks are left?**  
[[☃ input-number 1]]

$7$ mice were in a wagon.  
$3$ mice jumped out.
 
**How many mice are in the wagon now?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
$\blue{7}-\pink{3}=\green{4}$

$\green4$ mice are in the wagon now.

items.question_content:
Captain Blue saw $5$ whales.  
$2$ of the whales swam away.

**How many whales were left?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] whales
